story.iWant: To measure discussion and critical thinking skills from each session transcript
story.soThat: Learning trajectory can be tracked for discussion-led courses (Philosophy, Ethics, PSHE)

assumptions: Session involves open-ended dialogue on ethical, philosophical, or social topics
There is no single correct answer — quality is in reasoning, not conclusions
Tutor has posed questions that invite multiple perspectives

acceptanceCriteria.title: Perspective diversity scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student discussing an ethical or philosophical question
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses how many viewpoints they consider
acceptanceCriteria.then: A perspective diversity score between 0-1 is produced

acceptanceCriteria.title: Argument quality scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student making claims or defending a position
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses the logical structure of their reasoning
acceptanceCriteria.then: An argument quality score between 0-1 is produced

acceptanceCriteria.title: Position shift scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student challenged on their views during discussion
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses whether they revise or refine their position
acceptanceCriteria.then: A position shift score between 0-1 is produced

acceptanceCriteria.title: Reflection scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student in dialogue about their own thinking
acceptanceCriteria.then: A reflection score between 0-1 is produced

failureConditions.implication: Not enough student speech to assess discussion skills — use neutral defaults

parameters.name: perspective_diversity
parameters.description: Considers multiple viewpoints rather than holding a single rigid position

definitions.high: Actively considers 3+ viewpoints, including ones they disagree with
definitions.low: Only considers their own position, dismisses alternatives
Oversimplifies, sees issues as purely right/wrong

definitions.high: Represents opposing views accurately and charitably
definitions.low: Strawmans or dismisses opposing views without engagement

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Single rigid viewpoint, no awareness of alternatives

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Acknowledges alternatives exist but doesn't engage

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Considers some perspectives but defaults to own view

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Genuinely weighs multiple perspectives with nuance

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Actively seeks and steelmans opposing views

scoringAnchors.example: That's just wrong. There's no way to justify it.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Dismisses alternative views outright, no engagement with other perspectives

scoringAnchors.example: I mean, I guess some people think differently, but I still think I'm right.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Acknowledges others disagree but doesn't engage with their reasoning

scoringAnchors.example: I see the other side — they'd say that freedom matters more than safety. But I think safety has to come first because...
scoringAnchors.rationale: States the opposing view and responds to it, though prioritises own conclusion

scoringAnchors.example: There are at least three ways to look at this. A utilitarian would say maximise happiness for the most people. A rights-based approach would say the individual's rights can't be violated regardless. And a virtue ethicist might ask what a good person would do. Each has merits.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies multiple frameworks, represents each fairly, doesn't rush to conclusion

scoringAnchors.example: The strongest version of the argument I disagree with would be... [steelmans it]. And even though I end up somewhere different, I think they're right about [specific point]. The tension between these views is actually where the interesting question is.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Steelmans opposing view, finds partial agreement, locates productive tension

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student already considers multiple perspectives. Push toward evaluating frameworks: 'Which of these perspectives do you find most compelling and why?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student is locked in one view. Present contrasting perspectives: 'Some people would argue the opposite — that X. What would you say to them?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student is open but surface-level. Deepen engagement: 'You said the other side thinks X. Why might they believe that? What's their strongest argument?'

parameters.name: argument_quality
parameters.description: Constructs reasoned arguments with premises, evidence, and logical connection to conclusions

definitions.low: Assertions without structure or logical flow

definitions.high: Logical connections, avoids fallacies, addresses counterarguments
definitions.low: Circular reasoning, non-sequiturs, or emotional assertions

definitions.high: Uses examples, analogies, or principles to support claims

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Assertions without reasoning

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Attempts reasoning but weak structure

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Some logical structure but gaps in reasoning

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Well-structured arguments with supporting evidence

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Sophisticated argumentation with counterargument awareness

scoringAnchors.example: It's just wrong because it is.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Pure assertion, no reasoning

scoringAnchors.example: I think it's wrong because people shouldn't do that kind of thing.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Circular reasoning, no specific support

scoringAnchors.example: I think it's wrong because it causes harm. For example, if everyone did it, society would suffer. So we should avoid it.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Basic consequentialist argument with example, but doesn't consider objections

scoringAnchors.example: My argument is: the right to privacy is fundamental because it protects autonomy. Without privacy, people self-censor, which undermines democracy. You might object that security requires surveillance — but I'd respond that targeted, warranted surveillance achieves security without blanket privacy invasion.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Clear structure, principled reasoning, anticipates and addresses counterargument

scoringAnchors.example: Let me distinguish two claims that are getting conflated. First, the empirical question: does X actually cause harm? The evidence suggests yes, but it's contested. Second, the normative question: even if it does, is preventing it worth the cost? These require different types of reasoning. On the first, I'd appeal to research. On the second, we need to weigh competing values.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Meta-analytical, distinguishes claim types, sophisticated philosophical reasoning

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student argues well. Introduce harder dilemmas: 'What if both options cause harm? How do you decide?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student asserts without reasoning. Model argument structure: 'What's your main reason? Can you give an example?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student has basic reasoning. Push for counterarguments: 'What's the strongest objection to your view?'

parameters.name: position_shift
parameters.description: Willingness and ability to revise or refine a position when presented with new information or arguments

definitions.high: Explicitly acknowledges when their view has changed and why
definitions.low: Rigidly defends original position regardless of new input

definitions.high: Revisions are substantive and reflect genuine engagement with the challenge
definitions.low: Superficial agreement ('yeah I guess so') without real integration

definitions.high: Distinguishes between changing their mind and being convinced by social pressure
definitions.low: Shifts position to please tutor rather than genuine reasoning

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Rigidly fixed — will not entertain change

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Acknowledges challenges but doesn't integrate them

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Sometimes revises but inconsistently

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Genuinely revises when good reasons are presented

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Actively seeks challenge, revises substantively, articulates why

scoringAnchors.example: No, I'm not changing my mind. I know what I think.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Refuses to engage with challenge

scoringAnchors.example: Yeah, OK, maybe. But I still think the same thing really.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Superficial acknowledgement, no real shift

scoringAnchors.example: Hmm, I hadn't considered that angle. I'm not sure I fully agree but it does complicate my position. Let me think about it.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Genuine uncertainty, acknowledges complication, doesn't rush to new conclusion

scoringAnchors.example: I think you've changed my mind on one specific thing — I was assuming X, but actually the evidence suggests Y. My overall position is similar but with an important caveat now.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Specific, substantive revision with clear reasoning about what changed and why

scoringAnchors.example: This is interesting — I came in thinking A, but after discussing B and C, I've realised my original reasoning was based on a flawed assumption. I now think D, because... But I want to flag that I'm less certain than before, and here's what would change my mind again.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Traces the journey of their thinking, articulates the revision process, maintains intellectual humility

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student revises thoughtfully. Present genuine dilemmas: 'What would it take to change your mind on this?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student is rigid. Don't force change — instead model uncertainty: 'I wonder if it's possible that both views contain some truth?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student is open but surface-level. Deepen: 'You said your view shifted — can you pinpoint exactly what caused the shift?'

parameters.name: reflection_quality
parameters.description: Metacognitive awareness — thinking about their own thinking

definitions.high: Notices their own biases, patterns, and reasoning habits
definitions.low: No awareness of how their background or assumptions shape their views

definitions.high: Explicitly identifies and questions their own assumptions
definitions.low: Treats assumptions as facts without examination

definitions.high: Articulates how their thinking has developed across sessions
definitions.low: No awareness of change in their own thinking

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: No metacognitive awareness

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Occasional 'I think...' without deeper reflection

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Sometimes notices own assumptions but inconsistently

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Regularly reflects on own thinking and assumptions

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Deep metacognition — tracks own intellectual growth

scoringAnchors.example: It's obvious. Everyone knows that.
scoringAnchors.rationale: No awareness of own assumptions, presents opinion as universal fact

scoringAnchors.example: I think this because... well, I've always thought this way.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Acknowledges it's personal but doesn't examine why

scoringAnchors.example: I notice I'm assuming that everyone values fairness the same way I do. Maybe that's not true across cultures.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies a specific assumption and questions its universality

scoringAnchors.example: I realise I keep coming back to consequentialist reasoning — that's probably because of how I was raised. It makes me wonder if I'm missing something that a deontological view would catch.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Meta-level awareness of own reasoning pattern, identifies its origin, sees limitation

scoringAnchors.example: Compared to where I started these sessions, my thinking has shifted significantly. I used to see this as black and white, but now I hold my views more tentatively. I'm not sure if that's growth or just confusion — but I think being comfortable with not knowing is itself a kind of progress.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Tracks intellectual growth across sessions, reflects on the nature of progress itself

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student is reflective. Push toward philosophical metacognition: 'Why do you think you tend toward that type of reasoning?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student doesn't reflect. Model it: 'I notice you said X with a lot of certainty. What's that certainty based on?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student occasionally reflects. Encourage it: 'You just identified an assumption — that's really valuable. Can you find another one in your reasoning?'
